content:
{
  "lens": "test-quality",
  "findings": [
    {
      "severity": "high",
      "category": "test-quality",
      "file": "test/auth.test.ts",
      "line": 34,
      "title": "Tests assert mock interactions instead of behavior",
      "description": "Tests like 'should create a new user with hashed password' verify that bcrypt.hash WAS CALLED (spy assertion) but not that the hash was stored correctly or with adequate cost factor. Test 5 checks bcrypt.hash was called but doesn't verify the cost factor argument — the very vulnerability present in production code (cost=4). The tests would pass even if the hashing was completely broken.",
      "suggestion": "Replace spy assertions with value assertions. Instead of expect(bcrypt.hash).toHaveBeenCalled(), test with a real bcrypt implementation and verify the output hash is valid. For integration scenarios, test the full register→login flow.",
      "confidence": 0.92
    },
    {
      "severity": "low",
      "category": "test-coverage",
      "file": "test/auth.test.ts",
      "line": 1,
      "title": "Zero negative and edge case tests",
      "description": "All 12 tests cover happy paths or trivial variations. Missing: empty password, extremely long password (bcrypt DoS), SQL injection characters in email, expired JWT, malformed JWT, concurrent session handling, unicode in names. The 94% line coverage metric is misleading — lines are executed but correctness is not verified.",
      "suggestion": "Add negative tests: empty inputs, boundary values (72-byte bcrypt limit), malicious inputs (SQL injection, XSS in name field), error scenarios (DB down, bcrypt failure). Each negative test should verify the error is handled safely.",
      "confidence": 0.90
    }
  ]
}
